entries.label: Emergent Modularity
entries.workingDef: A training objective and model property in which a Mixture-of-Experts architecture develops coherent, domain-aligned expert groupings without relying on predefined human labels. Achieved in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) by constraining all tokens within a document to route through a shared expert pool, so recurring expert groups self-organise from data structure rather than externally imposed categories. Enables selective deployment of small expert subsets for specific tasks while retaining near full-model performance.

entries.label: Document-Boundary Routing
entries.workingDef: A training technique introduced in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) in which all tokens within a single document are constrained to activate experts drawn from a shared, document-level expert pool. The pool is selected by averaging per-token router preferences across the document. This uses document boundaries as a weak, unlabelled supervisory signal to encourage higher-level domain specialisation in MoE experts, avoiding the need for explicit domain labels on the pretraining corpus.

entries.label: Selective Expert Use

Subset selection is guided by routing-usage statistics on a small validation sample. Enables improved memory and compute efficiency for specific tasks without separate fine-tuned models. Contrasts with standard MoE behaviour where full expert access is required to avoid significant performance degradation.

Standard MoE training tends to produce the latter; EMO's document-boundary routing is designed to produce the former. Linked to the broader goal of composable and interpretable model architectures.

entries.label: Benchmaxxing
entries.workingDef: The practice of optimizing a model specifically to improve its score on a known benchmark without achieving corresponding gains in real-world performance. Coined as a portmanteau of 'benchmark' and 'maxxing,' the term describes deliberate or incidental overfitting to public test sets — including training on data that closely resembles evaluation splits. Closely related to benchmark gaming and evaluation gaming, but implies an active, targeted optimization strategy rather than accidental saturation.

entries.label: Test-Set Contamination

In ASR and LLM contexts alike, contamination may be explicit (direct inclusion of test examples) or distributional (training on data from the same source or domain as the test set). Motivates the use of private or undisclosed evaluation sets.

entries.label: Wafer-Scale Engine
entries.workingDef: A chip architecture pioneered by Cerebras Systems in which the entire silicon wafer — rather than individual dies cut from it — is treated as a single integrated chip. By laying wiring across the scribe lines that normally separate reticle exposures, Cerebras eliminates chip-to-chip interconnects within the device, yielding extremely high on-chip SRAM and memory bandwidth. The WSE-3, for example, offers ~44GB of SRAM at ~21 PB/s bandwidth — roughly 6,000× the memory bandwidth of an H100 GPU — making it highly suited to memory-bandwidth-bound inference workloads, though its limited off-chip scaling constrains large-model training use cases.

entries.label: Prefill–Decode Split
entries.workingDef: A decomposition of LLM inference into two functionally distinct phases with different hardware requirements: (1) prefill, which encodes the full input context into an internal state and is highly parallelisable and compute-bound; and (2) decode, which autoregressively generates output tokens by reading both the KV cache (context) and model weights on every step, and is serial and memory-bandwidth-bound. The split has practical implications for chip selection — prefill favours high-compute GPUs while decode favours high-bandwidth, low-latency memory — and motivates heterogeneous or disaggregated inference architectures.

sources.title: The Inference Shift

entries.label: Heterogeneous AI Compute
entries.workingDef: A framing for the emerging AI infrastructure landscape in which no single chip architecture dominates all workloads. Contrasted with the 'GPU era' where Nvidia's GPUs handled both training and inference, heterogeneous compute argues that future AI deployments will mix purpose-built silicon — e.g., wafer-scale chips for low-latency inference, specialised training accelerators, edge chips — matched to specific workload profiles. The article treats Cerebras' IPO as a signal of this structural shift.

sources.title: The Inference Shift

entries.label: Inference Shift

The framing argues that as agent usage scales, inference becomes the primary driver of compute spend, creating market conditions for specialised inference silicon distinct from Nvidia's GPU-centric stack. As a named framing by a single analyst, its adoption and durability as industry terminology remains to be seen.

entries.label: Zombie Internet
entries.workingDef: A term coined by Jason Koebler to describe the current state of the internet as neither purely bot-driven (the 'Dead Internet' scenario) nor authentically human — but a hybrid ecology in which humans using AI interact with other humans using AI, humans interact with bots, and automated accounts interact with real people. The framing captures the cognitive and epistemic exhaustion of navigating spaces where the provenance of any given piece of content is genuinely unclear. Distinguished from 'Dead Internet Theory' in that the internet is not dead but animated by something ambiguous and harder to filter: zombie-like human-AI hybrids rather than pure bot-spam.

entries.label: Dead Internet Theory

Invoked here as the foil to the 'Zombie Internet' framing — the Dead Internet imagines a clean separation (bots vs. humans) whereas the Zombie Internet describes a messier, harder-to-detect hybrid. The term precedes the current AI content wave but has been revived as context for understanding AI's effect on online authenticity.

Distinct from hallucination (which is a model failure) and from AI-assisted writing (which may be high quality); slop specifically connotes volume-over-quality content farms. entries.label: AI-Generated Exploit
entries.workingDef: An exploit or malicious payload whose code shows evidence of having been drafted or refined using a large language model — for example, hallucinated CVSS scores, structured 'textbook' formatting consistent with LLM training data, or AI-optimised payload tuning. Google Threat Intelligence Group's May 2026 report is the first public instance of a major threat-intelligence organisation attributing a zero-day exploit to AI-assisted development. The category is distinct from AI-discovered vulnerabilities (where AI finds flaws in legitimate code); here AI is used by the attacker to construct the offensive payload. Forensic identification relies partly on LLM output artefacts embedded in the code.

entries.label: Persona-Driven Jailbreaking
entries.workingDef: A jailbreaking technique in which an attacker instructs an AI model to adopt a persona — such as a security expert — in order to elicit responses the model would otherwise refuse, such as vulnerability analysis or exploit generation. Documented by Google GTIG as an observed attacker pattern in 2026. Related to but distinct from generic prompt injection; persona-driven jailbreaking relies on role-framing rather than instruction overrides or data-plane attacks.

The hallucinated score is a characteristic LLM output artefact: the model generates plausible-looking metadata without verifying its accuracy. Its presence in a malicious script provides defenders with a new category of attribution evidence for AI-assisted attacks.

entries.label: Enterprise Philosophy

Thompson argues the correct historical analogy is the 1970s mainframe wave (accounting/ERP software eliminating back-office roles), not SaaS or the consumer internet.

entries.label: Unelevated Sandbox
entries.workingDef: A sandbox design pattern — specifically described in OpenAI's Codex Windows implementation — in which isolation constraints (file-write limits, network restrictions) are enforced without requiring the user to grant administrator (elevated) privileges. Contrasted with approaches that prompt for privilege escalation, the unelevated approach uses OS primitives such as write-restricted tokens and synthetic SIDs to enforce boundaries within the normal user permission level.

entries.label: Write-Restricted Token
entries.workingDef: A Windows process token variant that causes the operating system to perform an additional access check on write operations before allowing them to proceed. Used in sandbox implementations (such as OpenAI's Codex Windows sandbox) to limit which filesystem locations a sandboxed process can modify, without requiring elevated privileges or changes to the host filesystem's integrity labels.

sources.title: Building a safe, effective sandbox to enable Codex on Windows

entries.label: Process Token
entries.workingDef: A Windows security object associated with a running process that defines that process's identity, group memberships, and privileges. Process tokens determine what system resources and actions a process may access. In agentic coding contexts (e.g., Codex on Windows), process tokens are manipulated to create constrained execution environments without elevating or modifying host-filesystem metadata.

entries.label: Schedule Padding
entries.workingDef: The practice of deliberately adding extra time to published flight schedules to improve on-time performance (OTP) statistics without materially improving operational reliability. A form of metric gaming in which reported performance diverges from actual customer experience — airlines inflate scheduled durations so flights arrive 'on time' by the official definition even when nothing operationally improved.

entries.label: Customer-Centric Metrics

Contrasted with traditional operational metrics (e.g., on-time performance based on 15-minute cutoffs) that can be gamed or that fail to capture experience for heterogeneous customer segments — e.g., connecting passengers vs. non-stop passengers in the airline context.

entries.label: Operational Resilience

In the article's framing, resilience is positioned as compatible with — rather than opposed to — efficiency, when buffers are allocated predictively and metrics are designed around customer outcomes rather than operational proxies.

entries.label: Agentic Inference
entries.workingDef: A term used in Stratechery's 'The Inference Shift' (Thompson, 2026) to distinguish the class of AI inference where no human is in the loop — autonomous agents executing tasks without real-time human oversight — from 'answer inference', where humans wait for a response and latency is a primary constraint. The agentic inference framing is part of the broader contested 'agentic' framing: lab and industry marketing uses 'agentic' to name a new deployment paradigm, while critics (e.g., Kapoor & Narayanan) argue it outpaces measured capability and conflates multi-step LLM workflows with the formal RL meaning of 'agent.'

entries.label: Answer Inference
entries.workingDef: Thompson's (Stratechery, 2026) label for the currently dominant mode of AI inference: a human submits a query and waits for a response, making latency a primary optimization target. Contrasted with 'agentic inference', where no human is in the loop and throughput or cost may matter more than speed. The distinction is used to argue that compute infrastructure optimized for today's inference workloads may be poorly suited to future autonomous-agent workloads.

entries.label: Curb Cut Effect
entries.workingDef: The phenomenon — originating in disability studies and universal design literature — whereby accommodations designed for people with disabilities (e.g., curb cuts for wheelchair users) prove broadly beneficial to the general population. Increasingly cited in technology and AI product design discourse to argue that accessibility-first design generates features with wide adoption, such as voice recognition, caption tools, and ergonomic interfaces.

Proponents argue openness enables reuse, scrutiny, and accountability; critics or cautious practitioners point to vulnerability exposure as a reason to restrict access, as illustrated by the NHS's decision to close repositories following disclosures via Project Glasswing.

entries.label: AGI
entries.workingDef: A term contested across multiple communities. OpenAI/industry capability framings — prominently foregrounded in this article's trial evidence — use AGI to mean systems that broadly equal or surpass human knowledge and ability across economically valuable tasks, a threshold that served as the explicit founding motivation for OpenAI. Theoretical/academic lineages (Hutter, Goertzel) define it as generality across cognitive tasks at human level, a more abstract construct. Skeptics (Marcus, Bender, Mitchell) argue the term has no stable operational definition: each speaker defines it to fit their narrative and the goalposts shift with marketing cycles. The Musk v. Altman trial made the contestation concrete — both sides invoked AGI as the supreme motivating stake while disagreeing entirely about who should control it.

entries.label: AI Leadership Accountability
entries.workingDef: A framing — emerging from criticism of the AI industry's governance culture — for the question of whether the individuals and organisations directing frontier AI development are sufficiently trustworthy, honest, and structurally accountable to the public whose lives their systems affect. Used critically by journalists, AI ethics scholars, and governance researchers to challenge the assumption that technical or commercial success confers the legitimacy to make civilisation-scale decisions. The Musk v. Altman trial gave the concept renewed force: testimony from multiple senior insiders alleged a pattern of deception and power-seeking at the highest levels of the industry's leading lab.

entries.label: AI Governance Culture
entries.workingDef: A term used in journalism and critical AI discourse to describe the internal norms, power structures, and accountability mechanisms (or their absence) within AI organisations — distinct from formal external regulation. The Musk v. Altman trial surfaced specific allegations: safety reviews allegedly bypassed, board members switching allegiances, and executive decision-making described under oath as deceptive. The term captures how internal culture shapes whether stated safety commitments translate into actual practice, and is increasingly invoked as a lens for evaluating whether self-governance by AI labs is credible.

entries.label: SDK Generation
entries.workingDef: The automated process of producing Software Development Kits — language-idiomatic client libraries, CLIs, and connectors — from an API specification. Stainless, acquired by Anthropic in 2026, pioneered this approach, generating official Anthropic SDKs across TypeScript, Python, Go, Java, Kotlin, and more from a single spec source. In the agentic context, SDK generation is framed as infrastructure-layer work: agents are only as capable as the APIs they can reliably reach, making high-quality, automatically-maintained SDKs a prerequisite for robust agent connectivity.

entries.label: Agent Connectivity

Anthropic frames agent connectivity as a core constraint on agent capability — 'agents are only as useful as what they can connect to' — making SDK quality, MCP server tooling, and connector infrastructure first-class engineering concerns. The term marks a shift in framing from model capability alone toward the surrounding integration surface as the binding constraint on agent usefulness.
